en.extract:
The olive manakin (Xenopipo uniformis) is a species of bird in the family Pipridae. It is found in Brazil, Guyana, and Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The olive manakin was originally described as Chloropipo uniformis. It was later assigned to genus Xenopipo that Jean Cabanis had erected in 1847. It shares that genus with the black manakin (X. atronitens).
The olive manakin has two subspecies, the nominate X. u. uniformis (Salvin & Godman, 1884) and X. u. duidae (Chapman, 1929).


Description
The olive manakin is 13 to 13.5 cm (5.1 to 5.3 in) long and weighs 16.5 to 21 g (0.58 to 0.74 oz). Unusual for a manakin, the sexes have the same plumage. The species also has longer wings and tail relative to its size than many other manakins. Adults of the nominate subspecies have dark olive upperparts. Their throat and underparts are a slightly paler olive and their belly has a yellowish cast. Subspecies X. u. duidae is slightly smaller and brighter than the nominate. Both subspecies have a dark iris, bill, and legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The olive manakin has a highly disjunct distribution. The nominate subspecies has the largest contiguous range. It encompasses east-central Bolívar in eastern Venezuela and continues slightly into extreme northern Brazil's Roraima and somewhat further into west-central Guyana. The nominate is also found on tepuis in southern Bolívar. Subspecies X. u. duidae is found in Venezuela on Cerro Duida and Cerro Sipapo, tepuis in Amazonas. There are also a very few scattered records further south and east in Brazil, at least two of which are very distant from the species' core range.
The olive manakin inhabits humid and wet mossy forest and stunted woodland, especially areas heavy with Melastomataceae. In elevation it ranges between 800 and 2,100 m (2,600 and 6,900 ft) in Venezuela and up to 1,200 m (3,900 ft) in Brazil.


Behavior


Movement
The olive manakin is primarily a year-round resident though it is thought to possibly make sonme elevational movements.


Feeding
The olive manakin is known to feed on small fruits and probably also includes insects in its diet. Its foraging behavior has not been studied.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the olive manakin's breeding biology.


Vocalization
The olive manakin's "advertising call" is "a clear, rising whistle, preeeeeeeeEE, penetrating but not loud". It is sometimes preceded by "a few low, stuttery stu-tu-tu-tu-tu notes given quickly, or a short chip note".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the olive manakin as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common in Venezuela and rare in Brazil. It is found in Venezuela's Canaima National Park.


References

The '''olive manakin''' ('''''Xenopipo uniformis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Pipridae]]. It is found in [[Brazil]], [[Guyana]], and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/cotingas/ |title=Cotingas, manakins, tityras, becards |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The olive manakin was [[Species description|originally described]] as ''Chloropipo uniformis''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Salvin |first=Osbert |last2=Godman |first2=Frederick DuCane | journal=Ibis | title=XLVI.—Notes on Birds from British Guiana | volume=II | issue=5 | pages=447 | date= 1884 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8526808 | access-date=October 13, 2025 }}</ref> It was later assigned to genus ''[[Xenopipo]]'' that [[Jean Cabanis]] had erected in 1847.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Cabanis |first=Jean |title=Ornithologische Notizen | date= 1847 | journal=Archiv für Naturgeschichte | publisher=Nicolai | volume=1 |pages=235–236 |language=German | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14785205 |access-date=October 13, 2025 }}</ref> It shares that genus with the [[black manakin]] (''X. atronitens'').<ref name=IOC15.1/>

The olive manakin has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''X. u. uniformis'' ([[Osbert Salvin|Salvin]] & [[Frederick DuCane Godman|Godman]], 1884) and ''X. u. duidae'' ([[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1929).<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The olive manakin is {{convert|13|to|13.5|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|16.5|to|21|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. Unusual for a manakin, the sexes have the same plumage. The species also has longer wings and tail relative to its size than many other manakins. Adults of the nominate subspecies have dark olive upperparts. Their throat and underparts are a slightly paler olive and their belly has a yellowish cast. Subspecies ''X. u. duidae'' is slightly smaller and brighter than the nominate. Both subspecies have a dark iris, bill, and legs and feet.<ref name=OLMA-BOW>Snow, D. (2020). Olive Manakin (''Xenopipo uniformis''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.oliman2.01 retrieved October 13, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The olive manakin has a highly [[disjunct distribution]]. The nominate subspecies has the largest contiguous range. It encompasses east-central [[Bolívar (state)|Bolívar]] in eastern Venezuela and continues slightly into extreme northern Brazil's [[Roraima]] and somewhat further into west-central Guyana. The nominate is also found on [[tepui]]s in southern Bolívar. Subspecies ''X. u. duidae'' is found in Venezuela on [[Cerro Duida]] and Cerro Sipapo, tepuis in [[Amazonas (Venezuelan state)|Amazonas]].<ref name=OLMA-BOW/><ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | pages =662 | language =English }}</ref> There are also a very few scattered records further south and east in Brazil, at least two of which are very distant from the species' core range.<ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =278–279 |isbn=978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

The olive manakin inhabits humid and wet mossy forest and stunted [[montane forest|woodland]], especially areas heavy with [[Melastomataceae]].<ref name=OLMA-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/> In elevation it ranges between {{convert|800|and|2100|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Venezuela and up to {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Brazil.<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The olive manakin is primarily a year-round resident though it is thought to possibly make sonme elevational movements.<ref name=OLMA-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The olive manakin is known to feed on small fruits and probably also includes insects in its diet. Its foraging behavior has not been studied.<ref name=OLMA-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the olive manakin's breeding biology.<ref name=OLMA-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Xenopipo-uniformis |species=the olive manakin}}
===Vocalization===

The olive manakin's "advertising call" is "a clear, rising whistle, ''preeeeeeeeEE'', penetrating but not loud". It is sometimes preceded by "a few low, stuttery ''stu-tu-tu-tu-tu'' notes given quickly, or a short ''chip'' note".<ref name=Hilty/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the olive manakin as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common in Venezuela and rare in Brazil.<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=vanPerlo/> It is found in Venezuela's [[Canaima National Park]].<ref name=OLMA-BOW/>
